Case title: Controlled agent-based repair of broken social-science code packages
Short description: Inject realistic failures into reproducible social-science code packages and compare prompt-based repair with autonomous agent repair.
Empirical unit of analysis: Broken R-based social-science studies in clean Docker environments
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; shrinking routine repair labor
Key typology question: Is routine post-publication repair becoming automatable even if claim adjudication is not?
Observable artifacts / traces: Error logs, patch files, rerun traces, success/failure by complexity level
Key outcomes observed: Repair success rate, time saved, error complexity sensitivity
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Agent-based repair fails broadly or still requires dense manual debugging
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Controlled failure injection is cleaner than real-world package decay
Why this case has high leverage: A clean test of whether some technical reproducibility labor should be demoted to a shrinking task-cluster.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
